sentences.text: Yes, sure.
As I have told you, Star Street, which branched off from the center of town at Main, Main Street, was for a long stretch almost completely Jewish.
And perhaps, I can give you an indication of an interesting element of the economy of the Jewish community.
On the other side of Star Street, the first building was an apothecary, of course owned by Jews.
Because the apothecary, the, the chemist, as they would be called-- and of course, was a professional.
And therefore, he was a Jew.
With the rarest exception, so were all the doctors and lawyers.
And then next to it, you would have, for instance, the beadle living across the street from the main synagogue, which was known as the shul.
And he was, so to say, the custodian of the, of the main shul.

The Jewish community itself, it was almost a kind of a closed economy, where the, the community, to serve its religious and communal needs, in effect was a very substantial employer, and where em-- employment almost created a, a kind of a dynasty.
If you were the beadle of the, of the shul, and you eventually had your son or son-in-law take over for you.

If you were a shohet or, or a ritual slaughterer, as for instance, was a Mechel in our town, then when you married off one of your daughters toward a Gimpel or maybe it was a, a Mechel who was the, the son-in-law and then Gimpel was the father-in-law.
I don't know exactly at this point.
Then, in effect, your dowry for your daughter was your becoming also a ritual slaughterer in town.
And if you were the one who maintained the [NON-ENGLISH], then your, your son-in-law or your son eventually took over that position.
Some of these people would have either several functions or have trade or businesses besides.
For instance, the beadle of, of the shul, a certain Mr.
Hirsch, who at one time was a broom maker.
He had a factory for making brooms.
The factory, of course, consisted of himself, his children, with a few pieces of equipment.
And they made all the brooms for the whole town.
At another time-- I think it was the same Mr.
Hirsch or somebody else, for instance-- had a, a, a dairy plant.
I don't even know whether or not it can be dignified with the term plant.
But again, there were a few pieces of equipment which are used to separate milk and make butter and, and sour milk and things of that kind.
And then, you know, you would go down the street.
And even, for instance, what moving there was-- of course, moving was done by a big cart with some of these heavy horses that you occasionally see on old beer ads.
That was also Jewishly-owned-- and although the owner may have been a, a Jewishly-unlettered, he was an Orthodox or a member of an Orthodox community.

There hardware store, the letter store-- there, there were tradesmen who, certainly in the eye of the more Hasidic and Talmudically better-trained Jews would look down on the [YIDDISH SPEECH], the, the plain folk, nonetheless were, were members of a, of an Orthodox Jewish community.
And, you know, observed the rituals and the holidays and sometimes were quite self-possessed members of, of the synagogues and, and various institutions.
You could actually say that there was hardly any profession or any trade in which Jews did not participate and which were not a part of this closed economy.
I mean, there was a Jewish barber shop, or several of them.
And I don't think that you could go to a non-Jewish barber shop.
I don't even know whether there was in existence.
Or a corsetier-- it was all Jewish and Jewishly-owned.
The language of commerce was-- and, and the language of the street was, of course, Hungarian.

You have to know that the Hungarians were very insistent on Hungarianizing, not unlike the situation that we are familiar with here, although quite a number of the-- certainly, the more ultra-Orthodox Jews were very weak in their spoken Hungarian.
And some of them had no written Hungarian at all.

But by and large, of course, everybody spoke on Hungarian on the streets and on the-- and in, in the stores and workshops, including the Hasidic Jews who-- some of whom were merchants and who had to serve consumers and the clientele with what-- whatever Hungarian they could manage.
Or they had employees who, of course, who spoke Hungarian much better.
Because by and large, sales personnel were not the people who went to cheder or, or to yeshiva.
So they were more, shall I say, worldly.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But this-- this dynastic and self-perpetuating and kind of self-contained economy was really one of the main characteristics of the Jewish community in Kisvarda and certainly of other communities, too-- perhaps not to the same degree.

On the other hand, it wasn't only-- the Jews obviously did not restrict themselves to their own community in their commerce and in their trade so that institutions or, or enterprises that were of a more general nature, may have been in-- not may have been-- actually were in Jewish hands.
But so the public at large were not restricted to the Jewish community in the services or, or the goods that they sold.
Sure.
Well, in the Jewish school, of course, the teaching language was Hungarian.
It was not coeducational.
They had separate classes for boys and girls.
And of course, in the, in the state schools or the state-operated schools, it was, of course, again Hungarian of the teaching language.
But for the more orthodox element and not only the Hasidim, the spoken language of the house and the family language was usually Yiddish.
It-- you could almost judge the degree of religiosity by the, the language that was spoken at-- in the house.
There were the Hasidim whose real mother tongue was Yiddish.
And then as you went, so to say, up the ladder of-- or down the ladder of relative religiosity, the Yiddish could sort of peter out.
There would be occasional use or just a few words.
Until you got to be assimilated, who didn't know any Yiddish at all.
They may have understood some of it, because many of them would have had either a German education or knew German as a second language.
So they would understand the Yiddish.
But basically, you would almost judge the degree of the religiosity by the language used in the home.
The structure itself is in existence when I last visited there maybe about five or six years ago.
The, the fence around it, which was really part of the structure and was a rather elegant sort of fence and gate built into it was gone.
The institution, the, the structure itself stood.
The interior apparently was virtually totally destroyed in, in the sense that it was beautifully hand-painted.
And of the painting, there was a little bit left in the ceiling.
But the synagogue itself apparently has gone through several transformations, the last being a kind of a folk or, or-- what is that called?
It was a kind of a folkloristic exhibit and cultural house at the time I was there.
They had an exhibit of the history of Kisvarda with very large segments of it, of course, showing the history of the Jewish community.
Because the Jewish moment was of such importance in, in the history of the city.
As best as I understand it, the, the main shul is still, at this point, some kind of a cultural institution.
When did it stop being a synagogue?

Well, it really stopped being a synagogue when the Germans invaded Hungary and within a matter of a few weeks after that, the persecution of the Jews that was the Holocaust, not, not the ordinary persecution which the Jews have-- of Hungary had to endure virtually from 1919 on.
I would say that, curiously enough, I had a big hand in, in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the cessation of the shul being a house of prayer and a house of God.

Because on the second day of Pesach in 1944, shortly after the Germans marched into Hungary on March 19, 1944, I believe-- on that day, suddenly at the corner of, of Star and Main Street, were a line of carts appeared with gendarmes who then somehow almost immediately disappeared with Jews from the neighboring villages piled up, young and old, with a little bit of their belongings.
And they were just dropped there across the street from the synagogue, from the shul, which was only the second or third building out of the street-- at the beginning of the street.
And there they were, totally lost, obviously being uprooted suddenly and without-- not only not given any preparation, but totally lost.
Because they were given no idea of what their fate was to have been.
And in, in our town by that time, there were very few young Jewish men around.

Because most of the others had been hauled away long before to the so-called labor auxiliaries of the Hungarian army, which basically meant really being cannon fodder or, or mine detectors for the Germans in-- on the Eastern Front.
I must have been one of maybe a dozen young Jews of, of my age, with the exception of perhaps some of the more Hasidic Jews, who somehow managed to escape being called up to the labor auxiliary.
I wasn't called up, because I was registered at some other place.
And, and then they-- eventually, they transferred my registration to Kisvarda.
And in Kisvarda by that time, they weren't calling up people.
Because obviously, we were already consigned to annihilation.

But some of the other young people of my age whose registration was in some other town, particularly in Budapest, they were called up, so that in effect, we have had no able-bodied young Jews in the city with really exception of maybe a dozen or, or, or maybe two dozen people.

And when I say able-bodied, I don't even mean people who were physically able-bodied but who were mentally [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in, in the position and had the attitude of attempting to try to help.
And so I rustled up a couple of my peers and began to try to see what we could do for these unfortunate people who were simply dropped on Star Street.
And the only immediate roof that I could think of was, of course, the synagogue.

And I had terrible difficulty in attempting to get to the authorities that be to open the synagogue and to let us bring these people under the roof, to the point where eventually, we just had to break down the doors.
And then we had considerable difficulty with the reluctance of the people to desecrate the, the synagogue.
And of course, you also had difficulty with men and women being accommodated under the same roof and how you separate them, and then attempting to try to provide some means of, of hygiene.
You know, there were outhouses.
But they certainly weren't prepared for that kind of a, of a massive number of people.
And, and then the, the question of feeding them arose.
And remember, this was Pesach.
And the problems we have had because, essentially, the gendarmerie, which seems to have been almost exclusively in charge of the operation, weren't very kindly disposed on the one hand.

And on the other hand, the Jews of the city, being completely bewildered and lost, on the one hand, were afraid to have contact with the people who were brought in lest it have a deleterious impact on their existence.
They were still in their homes.

And as far as they were concerned, perhaps this transport of people that were brought in were brought in from the neighbor-- from the, the villagers because eventually, that-- those areas were going to become part of the, of the front line.
And maybe Jews were considered unreliable.
But nothing is going to happen to them.
And if they have too much of a contact with them, maybe it will reflect upon them.
So to persuade people to give money and food and, and dishes and clothing was a very difficult operation for the few able-bodied-- I, I should even say able-minded-- youngsters who are around.
And this went on for, for days.
I really don't know exactly for how long, because the operation of bringing the Jews in from the neighboring villages was just-- seemed almost haphazard as, as far as the authorities were concerned.
I'm sure it wasn't, but they just brought them in and plunked them down.

There were no preparations of any kind that were made for them, so that it was up to the few people like myself to attempt to bring some order and to try to at least prevent these people who were brought in from dying of starvation, from dying of exposure, from dying of hunger.
It, it was a, a, a hell of a mess.
You know, the next transport that came in, we went to a, to a man by the name of [INAUDIBLE] who had-- on Star Street-- a big lumber yard.
And he had a huge open shed that only had a roof and no sides.
And incidentally, Mr.
[INAUDIBLE] was, I think, one of two Jews who remained in Kisvarda who was not deported, because he was, I think, an American citizen.
There was one other man who was allowed to stay because he was 100% a war veteran, a war invalid from the First World War.
And so we brought the people in there.

And then to try to organize, again, the minimal hygiene or to, within a matter of days, to persuade women, young women particularly, to cut their hair, because there was an outbreak of lice right away, to try to provide enough water.
You have to understand, there was no hot and cold running water.
And if there was an outhouse in the back of a, of, of this lumber yard, that-- there were maybe 1,000 people around.
And so you had to provide some other means.
It was a-- an unbelievable undertaking, which was literally unorganized and unrecognized by the Jewish community authorities in the beginning.

Eventually, when our turn came or a few days before that, the ghetto was established on Star Street about the, the side-- on both sides of the street where the ritual bath was or where the Jewish community center was.
I, I don't mean by center a center like here.

What I mean is the-- where the offices were of the council and where the [INAUDIBLE] lived and where the entrance was to the, to the yard within which the shul wa-- the [INAUDIBLE] was and where all of these functionaries lived.

And on both sides of Star Street, to a depth of about, I would say, a block or a block and a half until the next street, they squeezed the Jews into the homes of the Jews who had lived there in normal times.
And then it became somewhat more organized.
And the-- the Jewish leadership of Kisvarda, in effect, was transformed into the Jewish council.
And then there was a, a Jewish so-called police established.
And there was some allotment of food-- very minimal-- so that immediately, we had to begin to organize some kind of a, an underground food supply.
And of course, there was no medication.
And immediately, we had to begin to organize that.

And then there was, of course, a, a skyrocketing death rate immediately, including a considerable number of suicides on the part of the more assimilated Jews or even converted-- the few converts that existed in-- that lived in Kisvarda.
And that became a serious problem.
Sure.
